audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 14, first-pass draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Joab's scripted widow, blood-revenge narrative, royal flattery, manipulation, return without accountability, appearance praise, hair weight, daughter Tamar, prolonged exclusion, arson, death-or-access demand, and performative reconciliation.

verses.restored: Joab son of Zeruiah knew heart of king was toward Absalom.

Joab sent to Tekoa and brought from there a wise woman. He said, Please present yourself as a mourner, wear mourning garments, do not anoint with oil, and be like a woman mourning many days for the dead.
Come to king and speak to him according to this word. Joab placed words in her mouth.
Woman of Tekoa spoke to king, fell on her face to ground, bowed, and said, Save, O king.
King said to her, What is it with you? She said, Truly I am a widow; my husband is dead.
Your maidservant had two sons. Two struggled in field, and no one separated them. One struck the other and killed him.

Look, whole clan has risen against your maidservant and says, Give the one who struck his brother, that we may put him to death for life of his brother whom he killed, and destroy heir also. They would extinguish my remaining coal and leave my husband neither name nor remnant upon face of ground.
King said to woman, Go to your house, and I will command concerning you.
Woman of Tekoa said to king, Upon me, my lord king, be guilt, and upon house of my father; king and his throne are innocent.
King said, Whoever speaks to you, bring him to me, and he shall not touch you again.

She said, Please let king remember YHWH your Divine, so avenger of blood does not increase destruction and they do not destroy my son. He said, As YHWH lives, not one hair of your son shall fall to ground.
Woman said, Please let your maidservant speak a word to my lord king. He said, Speak.
Woman said, Why then have you planned such a thing against people of Divine? By speaking this word king is like one guilty, in not returning his banished one.
We surely die and are like water spilled to ground that cannot be gathered. Divine does not take away life but devises means so banished one is not banished from Divine.
Now I came to speak this word to my lord king because people made me afraid. Your maidservant said, I will speak to king; perhaps king will perform word of his maidservant.
For king will listen, to rescue his maidservant from hand of man seeking to destroy me and my son together from inheritance of Divine.
Your maidservant said, Let word of my lord king give rest, because like messenger of Divine is my lord king to hear good and evil. May YHWH your Divine be with you.
King answered and said to woman, Please do not hide from me a thing I ask you. Woman said, Please let my lord king speak.

King said, Is hand of Joab with you in all this? Woman answered, By life of your soul, my lord king, no one can turn right or left from all my lord king has spoken. Your servant Joab commanded me and placed all these words in mouth of your maidservant.
To turn face/form of matter your servant Joab did this thing. My lord is wise like wisdom of messenger of Divine, knowing all that is in land.
King said to Joab, Look, I have done this thing. Go and return young man Absalom.
Joab fell on his face to ground, bowed, and blessed king. Joab said, Today your servant knows I found favor in your eyes, my lord king, because king performed request of his servant.
Joab arose, went to Geshur, and brought Absalom to Jerusalem.
King said, Let him turn to his house, and he shall not see my face. Absalom turned to his house and did not see face of king.
In all Israel there was no man greatly praised for beauty like Absalom. From sole of his foot to crown of his head there was no blemish in him.
When he cut hair of his head—at end of days he cut it because it was heavy upon him—he weighed hair of his head: two hundred shekels by royal weight.
To Absalom were born three sons and one daughter, whose name was Tamar. She was a woman beautiful in appearance.
Absalom lived two full years in Jerusalem and did not see face of king.
Absalom sent for Joab, to send him to king, but Joab was not willing to come. He sent again a second time, but Joab was not willing to come.
He said to his servants, See, Joab's field is near mine and he has barley there. Go and set it on fire. Servants of Absalom set field on fire.
Joab arose, came to Absalom at house, and said, Why did your servants set my field on fire?

Absalom said to Joab, Look, I sent to you, saying, Come here, that I may send you to king, saying, Why did I come from Geshur? It was better for me to still be there. Now let me see face of king; if guilt is in me, let him put me to death.
Joab came to king and told him. He called Absalom; he came to king and bowed on his face to ground before king, and king kissed Absalom.

translation_notes: The wise woman is recruited, costumed, and scripted by Joab. Her rhetorical skill is real, but the situation is engineered by a military commander.

The parable raises legitimate concerns about retaliatory family killing and preserving a widow's household; its application to Absalom is imperfect because Amnon's killing followed Tamar's rape and David's inaction.
Reconciliation is pursued without recorded consultation with Tamar, public truth, accountability for Amnon's rape, accountability for Absalom's execution, or repair for affected servants.
The woman repeatedly flatters David as angelic and all-knowing. Praise under royal power should not be mistaken for objective moral evaluation.
Absalom's return without access creates prolonged ambiguous punishment rather than a transparent justice process.
Praise of Absalom's body and hair is political image-making, not evidence of virtue. 'No blemish' language must not support ableism or beauty hierarchy.
Absalom names his daughter Tamar, preserving the name of his sister, but the text does not tell us what restoration the first Tamar received.
Burning Joab's field is coercive property destruction. Being ignored does not make arson a healthy communication method.
The final kiss is symbolic reunion without evidence of resolved conflict, responsibility, trust, or safety.
safety_note: Do not use this chapter to force family reconciliation, bypass survivors, praise charismatic beauty, or substitute symbolic affection for accountability. Wise mediation must not be scripted to protect dynastic stability at the expense of Tamar's voice and public justice.
